corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: Therefore, we present one of the first works in building a pre-trained vision-and-language cross- modality framework and show its strong perfor- mance on several datasets.

context_window.previous: Despite these influential single- modality works, large-scale pretraining and fine- tuning studies for the modality-pair of vision and language are still under-developed.
context_window.current: Therefore, we present one of the first works in building a pre-trained vision-and-language cross- modality framework and show its strong perfor- mance on several datasets.
context_window.next: We name this frame- work “LXMERT: Learning Cross-Modality En- coder Representations from Transformers” (pro- nounced: ‘leksmert’).

location.sentence: In order to better learn the cross-modal alignments between vision and language, we next pre-train our model with five diverse representative tasks: (1) masked cross- modality language modeling, (2) masked object prediction via RoI-feature regression, (3) masked object prediction via detected-label classification, (4) cross-modality matching, and (5) image ques- tion answering.

context_window.previous: It consists of three Trans- former (Vaswani et al., 2017) encoders: an object relationship encoder, a language encoder, and a arXiv:1908.07490v3 [cs.CL] 3 Dec 2019 cross-modality encoder.
context_window.current: In order to better learn the cross-modal alignments between vision and language, we next pre-train our model with five diverse representative tasks: (1) masked cross- modality language modeling, (2) masked object prediction via RoI-feature regression, (3) masked object prediction via detected-label classification, (4) cross-modality matching, and (5) image ques- tion answering.
context_window.next: Different from single-modality pre-training (e.g., masked LM in BERT), this multi-modality pre-training allows our model to infer masked features either from the visible ele- ments in the same modality, or from aligned com- ponents in the other modality.

top_candidates.sentence: Be-sides previous state-of-the-art (SotA) methods, we also show the human performance and imageonly/language-only results when available.
VQA The SotA result is BAN+Counter in , which achieves the best accuracy among other recent works: MFH , Pythia , DFAF , and Cycle-Consistency .
Thus our framework, with novel encoders and cross-modality pre-training, is suitable and achieves a 4.6% improvement on opendomain questions ('Open' in Table ).

Another evaluation metric consistency measures the proportion of unique sentences for which all related image pairs 8 are 5 These are state-of-the-art methods at the time of our EMNLP May 21, 2019 submission deadline.
MCAN (VQA challenge version) uses stronger mixture of detection features and achieves 72.8% on VQA 2.0 test-standard.
MUAN achieves 71.1% (compared to our 72.5%).

Via careful design and combination of these self-attention and cross-attention layers, our model is able to generate language representations, image representations, and cross-modality representations from the inputs.
We use three datasets for evaluating our LXMERT framework: VQA v2.0 dataset , GQA (Hudson and Manning, 2019), and NLVR 2 .
VQA The SotA result is BAN+Counter in , which achieves the best accuracy among other recent works: MFH , Pythia , DFAF , and Cycle-Consistency .
5 LXMERT improves the SotA overall accuracy ('Accu' in Table ) by 2.1% and has 2.4% improvement on the 'Binary'/'Other' question sub-categories.
Our 3.2% accuracy gain over the SotA GQA method is higher than VQA, possibly because GQA requires more visual reasoning.
7 NLVR 2 NLVR 2 is a challenging visual reasoning dataset where some existing approaches fail, and the SotA method is 'MaxEnt' in .
However, with our novel pretraining strategies in building the cross-modality connections, we significantly improve the accuracy ('Accu' of 76.2% on unreleased test set 'Test-U', in Table ) by 22%.
8 Each statement in NLVR 2 is related to multiple image pairs in order to balance the dataset answer distribution. correctly predicted.
We thus propose the LXMERT (Learning Cross-Modality Encoder Representations from Transformers) framework to learn these vision-and-language connections.
We thus propose the LXMERT (Learning Cross-Modality Encoder Representations from Transformers) framework to learn these vision-and-language connections.
After fine-tuning from our pretrained parameters, our model achieves the state-of-the-art results on two visual question answering datasets (i.e., VQA and GQA).
After fine-tuning from our pretrained parameters, our model achieves the state-of-the-art results on two visual question answering datasets (i.e., VQA and GQA).

Lastly, we demonstrate detailed ablation studies to prove that both our novel model components and pretraining strategies significantly contribute to our strong results; and also present several attention visualizations for the different encoders.
Therefore, we present one of the first works in building a pre-trained vision-and-language crossmodality framework and show its strong performance on several datasets.
Therefore, we present one of the first works in building a pre-trained vision-and-language crossmodality framework and show its strong performance on several datasets.
We name this framework "LXMERT: Learning Cross-Modality Encoder Representations from Transformers" (pronounced: 'leksmert').
This framework is modeled after recent BERT-style innovations while further adapted to useful cross-modality scenarios.
Our model outperforms previous works in all question categories (e.g., Binary, Number, Open) and achieves state-of-the-art results in terms of overall accuracy.
Our model outperforms previous works in all question categories (e.g., Binary, Number, Open) and achieves state-of-the-art results in terms of overall accuracy.
